title: The unequal work of staying in touch
summary: Analyse why the effort of keeping a long-distance friendship alive so often falls on one person, and what that imbalance reveals about how we measure love.
topic: Why the labour of staying in touch falls unevenly across distance
prompt: Write a 450–600-word analytical essay on why the work of keeping a long-distance friendship alive — remembering, reaching out, scheduling — tends to fall on one person, and what that reveals about how we judge who 'cares'. Give two concrete examples and concede one objection. Use cleft sentences, passive voice, and hedging.
hintStarters: What we call 'drifting apart' is often just one person quietly deciding to stop…
Granted, the friend who never writes first may value the friendship no less than…
It is the invisible work — the remembering, the scheduling across time zones — that…
We tend to measure caring by feeling, when distance measures it by…

hintVocab.meaningVi: tính toán hơn thua / ghi nhớ ai làm gì
đảm nhận vai trò dẫn dắt
bị bỏ rơi / lụi tàn dần
cảm thấy bị coi là điều hiển nhiên

hintVocab.phrase: out of sight, out of mind

hintVocab.meaningVi: để một tình bạn nguội lạnh
nhầm lẫn công sức với tình cảm

sampleText:
There is a particular kind of friendship death that leaves no body. No argument, no betrayal, no slammed door — just a thread of messages that one person keeps sending and the other keeps not answering, until even the sender stops. What we call 'drifting apart' is often just one person quietly deciding to stop carrying the friendship, and the other noticing, months later, that they had been carrying it alone.

This pattern is so common that it deserves a closer look. In almost every long-distance friendship, the labour of maintenance settles unequally. One person remembers the birthday, suggests the call, works out which hour is humane in two time zones; the other simply replies, warmly and gratefully, when contacted. It is the invisible work — the remembering, the scheduling across time zones, the first move repeated a hundred times — that keeps a distant friendship alive, and that work is rarely shared down the middle. When it stops being done, the friendship does not end loudly. It is allowed to lapse.

Consider two friends who left the same university for opposite ends of the world. For three years, one of them sent the messages, booked the calls, and flew the longer route to meet halfway. She told herself this was simply who she was — the organised one. What she was slower to admit was that organisation had quietly become obligation, and that being the one who always reaches out can curdle, over time, into the suspicion that one is the only one who cares. Or consider the reverse case: the friend who never writes first but answers every message with real warmth, and is genuinely wounded to be told he was absent. He was not absent in feeling. He was absent in labour, and from a distance the two are easily confused.

Granted, the friend who never writes first may value the friendship no less than the one who does, and it would be unkind to assume otherwise. People reach out at different rates for reasons that have nothing to do with love: some are buried in caregiving or shift work, some carry an anxiety about intruding, some simply never learned the habit. To read every unanswered message as indifference is to mistake a difference in temperament for a difference in devotion. The objection is fair and worth holding onto.

Even so, intentions are not the whole story, because a friendship is kept alive by actions, not by the warm feelings behind them. We tend to measure caring by feeling, when distance measures it by effort — and over enough miles, the feeling that never becomes a phone call is indistinguishable, from the other side, from no feeling at all. The friend who waited to be contacted may be hurt to learn this, but the hurt does not make it untrue.

What the imbalance finally reveals is how lazily we equate love with sentiment. Across a short distance, sentiment is enough; the friendship is held up by daily proximity, and no one has to keep the thread going on purpose. Across a long one, only deliberate effort will do — and the uncomfortable truth is that the person who keeps making that effort is not needier than the other. They are simply the only one still doing the work that distance has made impossible to fake.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Cleft sentences to expose the hidden cause
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'What we call drifting apart is often just one person … deciding to stop carrying the friendship' (what-cleft) và 'It is the invisible work … that keeps a distant friendship alive' (it-cleft) dùng cấu trúc chẻ câu để vạch ra nguyên nhân thật sự ẩn dưới một cách diễn đạt quen thuộc.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'What we call drifting apart is often just one person … deciding to stop carrying the friendship' (what-cleft) and 'It is the invisible work … that keeps a distant friendship alive' (it-cleft) use clefts to expose the real cause hidden beneath a familiar phrase.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Passive to depersonalise the slow ending
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'the friendship is kept alive by actions', 'It is allowed to lapse', và 'the work that distance has made impossible to fake' dùng bị động để biến cái kết của tình bạn thành một điều xảy đến hơn là một hành động có chủ ý, phản ánh cách mà nó thực sự 'lụi tàn' chứ không 'bị chấm dứt'.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'the friendship is kept alive by actions', 'It is allowed to lapse', and 'the work that distance has made impossible to fake' use the passive to turn the friendship's ending into something that happens rather than a deliberate act, mirroring how it really 'lapses' rather than being 'ended'.

sampleTranslationVi:
Có một kiểu chết của tình bạn không để lại xác. Không cãi vã, không phản bội, không cánh cửa đóng sầm — chỉ là một mạch tin nhắn mà một người cứ gửi đi và người kia cứ không trả lời, cho đến khi ngay cả người gửi cũng dừng lại. Cái mà chúng ta gọi là 'dần xa cách' thường chỉ là một người lặng lẽ quyết định ngừng gánh vác tình bạn, và người kia, nhiều tháng sau, nhận ra rằng họ đã gánh vác nó một mình.

Khuôn mẫu này phổ biến đến mức nó đáng được nhìn kỹ hơn. Trong hầu hết mọi tình bạn yêu xa, lao động của việc duy trì lắng xuống một cách không đồng đều. Một người nhớ ngày sinh nhật, đề nghị cuộc gọi, tính toán giờ nào là tử tế ở hai múi giờ; người kia chỉ đơn giản trả lời, một cách ấm áp và biết ơn, khi được liên hệ. Chính công việc vô hình — sự ghi nhớ, việc sắp xếp lịch qua các múi giờ, bước chủ động đầu tiên lặp lại cả trăm lần — mới giữ cho một tình bạn xa xôi sống động, và công việc đó hiếm khi được chia đôi. Khi nó ngừng được làm, tình bạn không kết thúc một cách ồn ào. Nó được để cho nguội lạnh dần.

Hãy xét đến hai người bạn rời cùng một trường đại học để đến hai đầu đối nghịch của thế giới. Suốt ba năm, một trong hai gửi những tin nhắn, đặt lịch các cuộc gọi, và bay theo lộ trình dài hơn để gặp nhau ở giữa đường. Cô ấy tự nhủ đây đơn giản là con người mình — người ngăn nắp. Điều cô chậm thừa nhận hơn là sự tổ chức đã lặng lẽ trở thành nghĩa vụ, và rằng việc là người luôn chủ động liên hệ có thể, theo thời gian, biến chua thành nỗi nghi ngờ rằng mình là người duy nhất quan tâm. Hoặc hãy xét trường hợp ngược lại: người bạn không bao giờ viết trước nhưng trả lời mọi tin nhắn với sự ấm áp thật sự, và thực sự bị tổn thương khi bị bảo rằng anh đã vắng mặt. Anh ấy không vắng mặt về mặt cảm xúc. Anh ấy vắng mặt về mặt công sức, và từ xa hai điều ấy dễ bị lẫn lộn.

Công nhận, người bạn không bao giờ viết trước có thể coi trọng tình bạn không kém gì người viết trước, và sẽ là không tử tế nếu giả định điều ngược lại. Người ta chủ động liên hệ ở những nhịp độ khác nhau vì những lý do chẳng liên quan gì đến tình yêu: một số người ngập trong việc chăm sóc người thân hay làm ca, một số mang trong mình nỗi lo ngại quấy rầy, một số đơn giản chưa bao giờ học được thói quen ấy. Hiểu mỗi tin nhắn không được trả lời là sự thờ ơ là nhầm lẫn một khác biệt về tính khí với một khác biệt về sự tận tụy. Phản đối này là công bằng và đáng để giữ lấy.

Dẫu vậy, ý định không phải là toàn bộ câu chuyện, bởi một tình bạn được giữ cho sống động bởi hành động, chứ không phải bởi những cảm xúc ấm áp đằng sau chúng. Chúng ta có xu hướng đo lường sự quan tâm bằng cảm xúc, trong khi khoảng cách đo lường nó bằng công sức — và qua đủ nhiều dặm đường, cảm xúc không bao giờ trở thành một cuộc gọi điện thì không thể phân biệt được, từ phía bên kia, với việc chẳng có cảm xúc nào cả. Người bạn chờ được liên hệ có thể bị tổn thương khi biết điều này, nhưng nỗi đau không làm cho điều đó trở thành sai.

Cái mà sự mất cân bằng cuối cùng hé lộ là chúng ta đánh đồng tình yêu với tình cảm một cách lười biếng đến thế nào. Qua một khoảng cách ngắn, tình cảm là đủ; tình bạn được nâng đỡ bởi sự gần gũi hằng ngày, và không ai phải cố tình duy trì mạch liên lạc. Qua một khoảng cách dài, chỉ có công sức có chủ ý mới đủ — và sự thật khó chịu là người liên tục bỏ ra công sức đó không phải là người thiếu thốn hơn người kia. Họ đơn giản là người duy nhất vẫn còn làm cái công việc mà khoảng cách đã khiến cho không thể giả vờ được.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a what-cleft to reinterpret a familiar phrase?
options: What we call 'drifting apart' is often just one person quietly deciding to stop carrying the friendship.
We call 'drifting apart' what is often just one person carrying the friendship.
Carrying the friendship is what we call drifting apart one person quietly.
Deciding to stop is what one person 'drifting apart' often we call carrying.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'What we call X is often just Y' is a what-cleft reframing the everyday phrase.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for 'What we call … is often just …'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses an it-cleft to name what really sustains a friendship?
options: The invisible work keeps a distant friendship alive which is the remembering.
It is the invisible work — the remembering, the scheduling across time zones — that keeps a distant friendship alive.
Keeping a friendship alive is what the invisible work the remembering does.
What keeps a friendship the invisible work it is the remembering alive.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'It is X … that …' is an it-cleft foregrounding the invisible work as the sustaining cause.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses the passive to depersonalise the slow ending?
options: They allow the friendship to lapse by it being done.
It is allowed to lapse.
Allowing it to lapse is what the friendship being done does.
Lapse the friendship allowed to be is by them it.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'It is allowed to lapse' uses the passive to make the ending something that happens, not a chosen act.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option uses 'is + past participle' without naming who acts?

options: Paid work in a caring profession
The unseen effort of remembering, reaching out, and sustaining a relationship
The cost of plane tickets to visit a friend
A strong feeling of affection
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Emotional labour' is the effort of maintaining relationships and managing feelings.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of 'labour' (work) applied to keeping a bond alive.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option introduces the counter-argument most naturally?
options: Some people are just selfish and lazy about their friends.
Granted, the friend who never writes first may value the friendship no less than the one who does.
Obviously anyone who doesn't text first clearly doesn't care at all.
Of course only organised people are capable of real friendship.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Granted, …' fairly concedes that the non-initiator may care just as much.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option admits the quiet friend may still love the friendship?

options: Being the one who reaches out always turns everyone into a bitter person.
Being the one who always reaches out can curdle, over time, into the suspicion that one is the only one who cares.
Being the one who reaches out will never affect how anyone feels.
Reaching out always the one who is curdle suspicion the only cares.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Can curdle, over time' allows for a tendency rather than asserting it always happens.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option uses 'can' and 'over time' to soften the claim?

mcQuestions.prompt: What is the essay's key distinction about the friend who never writes first?
options: He was absent in feeling, not in labour
He was absent in labour, not in feeling
He never cared about the friendship
He wrote first but was ignored
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay says 'He was not absent in feeling. He was absent in labour.'
mcQuestions.hint: Reread the end of the third paragraph.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence keeps a consistent analytical register?
options: Honestly some friends are just flaky and you have to deal with it.
A friendship is kept alive by actions, not by the warm feelings behind them.
Basically if they don't text you, they're just not that into you, simple.
It's kind of sad, you know, when a friendship just sort of fizzles out.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option B states the claim precisely; the others slip into casual speech.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which transition best pivots from concession back to the analysis?
options: In conclusion, feelings are all that matter.
Even so, intentions are not the whole story, because a friendship is kept alive by actions, not by the warm feelings behind them.
On the contrary, nobody ever has good intentions.
Anyway, that point is beside the point.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Even so, …' accepts the concession about intentions then advances the action-based claim.
mcQuestions.hint: Which phrase accepts the prior point and reasserts the thesis?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'to take for granted' mean here?
options: To reward someone generously
To assume someone's effort will always be there, without valuing it
To travel far to see someone
To end a friendship suddenly
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Take for granted' means failing to appreciate something assumed to be constant.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of not noticing effort because you expect it.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses parallel structure most effectively?
options: sent the messages, booked the calls, and flew the longer route
sending the messages and to book the calls and flying the route longer
the messages sent, booking the calls, and to fly route the longer
sent messages, the calls booking, and flew route longer the
mcQuestions.explanation: Three matched past-tense verb phrases form a clean parallel list.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option keeps all three verbs in the same tense and form?

mcQuestions.prompt: According to the essay, what does the imbalance ultimately reveal?
options: That the initiator is needier than the other friend
How lazily we equate love with sentiment rather than with effort
That long-distance friendships always fail
That feelings do not exist at a distance
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay says the imbalance reveals 'how lazily we equate love with sentiment.'
mcQuestions.hint: Reread the opening of the final paragraph.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'to let a friendship lapse' mean?
options: To formally end it with an argument
To let it quietly fade through neglect, without a clear break
To renew it with great effort
To report it to someone
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Lapse' means to gradually fall away or expire through inattention.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of something quietly fading rather than being cut off.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option best captures the essay's nuanced conclusion?
options: The friend who reaches out is always the better person.
Across distance, only deliberate effort sustains a friendship, and the one making it is not needier — just the only one still doing it.
Friendships at a distance never require any effort.
Feelings alone are enough to keep any friendship alive.
mcQuestions.explanation: The conclusion reframes the initiator's effort as the irreplaceable work distance demands, not neediness.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the option that defends the effort without condemning the other friend.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses an em-dash insertion correctly for a list of examples?
options: It is the invisible work — the remembering, the scheduling across time zones, the first move repeated a hundred times — that keeps a distant friendship alive.
It is the invisible work the remembering — the scheduling that keeps — a distant friendship alive across zones.
The invisible work — it is that keeps a friendship — the remembering scheduling alive.
Keeps a friendship alive — it is the invisible work — the remembering across time zones that.
mcQuestions.explanation: The dashes enclose a list of examples; remove them and the it-cleft remains grammatical.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option lets you delete the dashed list and keep a full sentence?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'one-sided' mean as used here?
options: Located on one edge of a map
Done or felt by one person far more than the other
Honest and open
Held in one language only
mcQuestions.explanation: 'One-sided' describes effort or feeling that flows mostly in one direction.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of only one person doing the giving.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Pick a friendship you keep across distance. Honestly, who reaches out first nine times out of ten? If it is always you, what would it mean to stop for a month — and if it is never you, what would the other person say you have been mistaking for love?
